sanskrit_devanagari:
यदृच्छया चोपपन्नं स्वर्गद्वारमपावृतम् ।
सुखिनः क्षत्रियाः पार्थ लभन्ते युद्धमीदृशम् ॥ ३२ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yadṛcchayā copapannaṁ svarga-dvāram apāvṛtam |
sukhinaḥ kṣatriyāḥ pārtha labhante yuddham īdṛśam || 32 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (IAST text body identical to vedabase)

exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.032 = MBh_06,024.032). Word-segmentation differs: GRETIL writes 'svargadvāram' as one word; modern editions write 'svarga-dvāram' with hyphen. Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: by chance / unsought / of its own accord
kṣatriyas / members of the warrior order
of this kind / such as this

translations.verbatim_quote: O Pārtha, happy are the kṣatriyas to whom such fighting opportunities come unsought, opening for them the doors of the heavenly planets.
O Parth, happy are the warriors to whom such opportunities to defend righteousness come unsought, opening for them the stairway to the celestial abodes.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As supreme teacher of the world, Lord Kṛṣṇa condemns the attitude of Arjuna, who said, 'I do not find any good in this fighting. It will cause perpetual habitation in hell.' Such statements by Arjuna were due to ignorance only.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.32 Only the fortunate Ksatriyas, i.e., the meritorious ones, gian such a war as this, which has come unsought, which is the means for the attainment of immeasurable bliss, and which gives an unobstructed pathway to heaven.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja stresses 'yadṛcchayā' (unsought) — the unsought arrival of the dharma-yuddha is itself evidence of accumulated merit (puṇya). Krishna is not telling Arjuna to seek battle; he is telling him that this particular battle has come to him without his seeking, and that arrival is itself a marker of fortune. The 'svarga-dvāram apāvṛtam' (door wide open) is read literally: the merit accruing from the dharma-yuddha is so direct that the door of svarga has no obstruction. Reading Krishna as exhorting belligerence misses the verse's force entirely — the verse marks the unsought-ness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Moreover, more than even the victors, those who die in a just battle attain happiness. By killing Bhisma and others, you make them happier. Even without doing karma yoga, one can attain svarga through the battle, without any obstructions (apavrtam).
commentaries.copyright_holder: Sri Vishvanatha Chakravarti / aggregated by bhagavad-gita.us
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Vishvanatha emphasises that the puṇya from a dharma-yuddha is independent of victory — even the warrior who dies in such a battle attains svarga. The 'apāvṛtam' (unobstructed) names this directness: no further intermediate practice is needed when the battle itself is dharmic. The reading aligns with Ramanuja and Sridhara on the unsought-arrival point, but adds the win-or-die symmetry that the verse contains.

positions.rendering: by accumulated merit, the battle has arrived without the warrior's seeking it; the arrival is itself evidence
manifested unsought of its own accord
without obstruction (apavrtam) — the door is open with no further effort required

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All sources render yadṛcchayā as 'unsought' or 'by chance / of its own accord.' The doctrinal disagreement is about what work the word is doing. Ramanuja reads it karmically — the unsought arrival of a dharma-yuddha is evidence of past merit. Vishvanatha reads it operationally — once such a battle has arrived unsought, one need not perform further karma-yoga to reach svarga; the battle itself is the path. Sridhara reads it rhetorically — 'why should you hesitate when the very thing you would have had to construct has already arrived?' Modern translators preserve 'unsought' but lose the doctrinal weight: the verse is not 'rejoice that war happened to you'; it is 'a fortuitous-arrival of the dharmic problem you would have had to seek out is itself a mark of fortune.' This is critical to avoid the militarism misreading.

literal_meaning: Happy are the kṣatriyas, O Pārtha, who obtain such a battle that has arrived unsought, with the door of heaven thrown wide open.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse names the unsought-arrival of a dharma-yuddha as a mark of fortune for the kṣatriya. Three doctrinal moves layer here: (a) yadṛcchayā — unsought-ness — is doing real work; the verse is not exhorting the seeking of battle but naming the recognition of an opportunity that has come without effort; (b) the dharma-yuddha is svarga-dvāra apāvṛta — a directly unobstructed path to merit, win or die — because the battle conforms to dharma, not because Krishna is glorifying war; (c) sukhinaḥ — happy / fortunate — is descriptive of the fortunate-state, not prescriptive of a desired emotion. Ramanuja, Vishvanatha, Sridhara, and Keshava Kashmiri converge on the unsought-ness as the load-bearing word. Reading the verse as 'go to war' misses 'yadṛcchayā' and inverts the verse: the verse marks the arrived-unsought, not the actively-sought.

remaining_gaps: Shankara is silent on this verse on bhagavad-gita.us (Madhva is also marked silent). Substantive Shankara reading not captured directly; Ramanuja + Vishvanatha + Sridhara provide cross-Sampradāya coverage.
